data.title: BND: When Diversification Becomes Diworsification
data.description:
Diversification is protection against ignorance. It makes little sense if you know what you are doing. - Warren Buffett

Peter Lynch famously coined the term "diworsification" to describe situations where spreading an investment too thin did not help reduce risks much, but did affect performance by adding subpar investments into the mix. The Vanguard Total Bond Market Index Fund ETF (NASDAQ:BND) is a great example, holding over 10,000 bonds, it basically guarantees mediocre returns. While it does have some advantages, like an extremely low expense ratio, it has also barely delivered any real returns since it was launched.

A large portion of the portfolio is in U.S. government or government-backed securities (Treasuries and agency mortgage-backed securities), with the remainder in high-grade corporate bonds. This means credit risk is low, but the ETF does have significant interest rate risk, as its effective average duration is almost six years. This means BND will likely face significant headwinds if interest rates continue to rise due to high inflation or elevated fiscal deficits. In other words, high inflation and rising yields are a significant risk for BND.

Historical Performance

Compared to the major categories of high-quality U.S. bonds, BND has underperformed all of them, a hint that "diworsification" could be at fault. It has even delivered lower total returns than the iShares 7-10 Year Treasury Bond ETF (IEF), whose average duration is relatively close. The biggest gap has been with the iShares Investment Grade Corporate Bond ETF (LQD), although in this case it could be argued that higher returns were to be expected to compensate for the added credit risk. The iShares TIPS Bond ETF (TIP) and the iShares 20+ Year Treasury Bond ETF (TLT) also delivered higher total returns, though these two ETFs carry higher duration risk.

Perhaps more concerning, BND's total return has barely outpaced the US Consumer Price Index. In other words, real returns have been less than 1%, not to mention the enormous underperformance compared to other defensive assets such as gold (GLD).

Portfolio Characteristics

BND provides exposure to the entire U.S. investment-grade bond market (Treasuries, agency mortgage-backed securities, and investment-grade corporate bonds) in one fund. Probably its two biggest selling points are the extremely low expense ratio of only 0.03% and the high liquidity given its massive assets under management (AUM). The BND ETF excludes high-yield “junk” bonds and emerging markets debt. This keeps credit risk low but also means BND’s income might lag funds that take more credit risk.

BND’s yield to worst is currently around 4.5%, the highest it has been in over a decade, thanks to the rise in interest rates. This yield roughly approximates the fund’s expected long-term return should yields stay at current levels. The average duration of ~5.8 years puts it firmly in the intermediate duration category.

The U.S. Dollar Factor

For an international investor, purchasing BND means investing in USD-denominated bonds, and therefore taking on currency exposure, unless they hedge it. The problem is that currency hedging can be expensive and often results in net yields close to the local-currency government bonds. For example, at the moment Japanese investors would have to pay around 4% annually to hedge their currency risk, resulting in final yields close to what long-term Japanese government debt offers. At the moment the U.S. trade-weighted dollar index is trading above its ten-year average, hinting at potential overvaluation.

Alternatives

While BND certainly offers a very simple approach to gain broad-market bond exposure at a low cost, we see some potential alternatives without taking significantly more risk.

For example, a combination of medium-term Treasuries, with IEF, and a small percentage in corporate bonds, with LQD, could result in higher total returns. A more tailored approach could be done depending on the risks that one wants to mitigate. For example, the Vanguard Short-term Inflation Protected ETF (VTIP) has a short interest rate duration, inflation protection, and excellent credit quality. Investors looking to avoid duration risk could consider options such as the iShares 0-3 Month Treasury Bond ETF (SGOV).

For those seeking an almost identical option from another provider, the iShares Core U.S. Aggregate Bond ETF (AGG) offers a very similar ETF, at basically the same cost, and with basically the same advantages and drawbacks. A more intriguing option is probably the Vanguard Total International Bond ETF (BNDX), which seeks broad exposure across major bond markets outside of the United States, and uses hedging strategies to mitigate exchange rate risk. Its expense ratio is slightly higher at 0.07%, but its yield-to-worst is currently a more attractive 4.9%.

Future Outlook

The current macroeconomic backdrop includes relatively high inflation, although below the peak reached in 2022, coupled with elevated fiscal deficits. There is also risk that inflation could be reignited by tariffs and increased fiscal deficits. On the positive side for BND investors, yields are the highest in years, implying better forward returns. This higher starting yield means future returns should be stronger than what investors experienced over the last decade of ultra-low rates.

For BND, an environment of persistent inflation and tight Fed policy could mean the ~4.5% yield could be offset by price losses if yields creep upward. If the U.S. fiscal deficit and debt issuance are large, this could keep long-term interest rates higher than they’d otherwise be, which would limit BND’s near-term upside or result in price declines.

Risks

When interest rates rise BND’s price usually declines to compensate. This rate-driven risk resulted in significant losses in 2022 as inflation overshot expectations, forcing the Fed to increase interest rates from near-zero to more than 4%. BND similarly tends to underperform in scenarios of rising government deficits and increased supply of bonds, which can steepen the yield curve and lift long-term rates. BND also has some corporate bonds, which adds some non-negligible credit risk. Still, BND tends to be significantly less volatile than assets like gold, which in recent decades experienced a draw-down almost twice the worst experienced by BND.

Takeaway

The BND ETF has shown that it is a low-cost, low-risk, and easy way to gain broad exposure to the bond market. Unfortunately, it has also shown that it offers low-rewards, with its total real returns barely in positive territory. As Buffett quipped, diversification is protection against ignorance. This means it could be a decent option for an investor that does not want to think too hard about fixed income.

Still, with BND underperforming most U.S. investment-grade bond categories, it feels less like smart diversification and more like the "diworsification" Peter Lynch warned us about. Investors are paying almost nothing in fees, but they are also not earning much in real terms. In a world where inflation is sticky and fiscal deficits are high, "owning everything" might not be such a great idea.

data.title: LVMH: Problems Mount Inside The Empire
data.description:
LVMH (OTCPK:LVMHF) shares are in a 45% drawdown, as the company suffers from declining demand for its once unattainable products. Meanwhile, scarcity-focused Hermes (OTCPK:HESAF) is not so far from its all-time highs.

As a result, for the first time in I don’t know how many years, Hermes surpassed LVMH to become the world’s highest valued luxury company. And that is quite symbolic.

What is going on? Is the LVMH empire falling or are we at an attractive entry point? Let’s find out.

Introduction

I’ve been covering LVMH on Seeking Alpha for a little over two years now. Back in 2023, luxury companies, including LVMH, have demonstrated extreme resiliency during a time almost all other sectors were declining in light of rising inflation, extreme valuations, and the burst of the ZIRP demand boom.

Initially, I was bullish on LVMH, expecting them to continue showing resiliency and growing. Then, around mid-2024, I changed my tone.

The stock is now down about 45% from its post-pandemic peaks. In my view, the reasons for the drawdown are in line with what I’ve warned back in my September article. In short, there are three big problems LVMH is dealing with.

One, its extraordinary growth during 2022-2023 benefitted meaningfully from aspirational customers. These customers aren’t that wealthy, but they had a lot of money during ZIRP. Once the macro deteriorated, they left, and LVMH doesn’t have enough “core” luxury demand to replace them.

Two, the core luxury customer wants scarcity. They want to buy clothes and accessories that make them feel elevated above the middle class. Once LVMH reached immense scale, it lost desirability in the eyes of said customers, who are the ones that should support the resiliency of the business on a year-to-year basis.

I’ll add that from my point of view, “loud” luxury in general seems to be going out of trend these days.

Three, operationally, costs got meaningfully higher, and there isn’t much more room to raise prices in light of the weaker demand and after the aggressive price increased during the highs of 2022-2023. Meanwhile, inventory levels are yet to normalize.

All these problems are clearly reflected in LVMH’s results.

First Quarter Numbers Erase Hopes For Recovery On Easier Comps

As LVMH shares started falling, the main debate was about whether the decline in growth is a matter of tougher comps, or is it a structural drop in demand. Initially, I was in the first camp, but I was quick and early to change my mind.

Last quarter, put a final blow to the tougher comps argument. LVMH is now fully lapping negative growth quarters with more negative growth.

Wines & Spirits have seen negative organic growth in 7 of the last 9 quarters, and the decline is worsening. Fashion & Leather has five consecutive quarter of organic growth lower than 2%, and three consecutive negative quarters. Perfumes & Cosmetics turned negative last quarter, while Watches & Jewelry zeroed on negative comps. Even Selective Retailing, which was the bright spot led by Sephora, turned negative in the first quarter.

Looking at geographies, Asia has seen double-digit declines for 4 consecutive quarters, Japan and the US turned negative, and Europe has seen low-single digit growth since Q4-23.

The problem for a business like LVMH, is that it has very significant fixed costs, which are quite difficult to cut, especially as an immediate reaction. As a result, the declines in sales are dragging down margins, taking LVMH almost all the way back to pre-pandemic levels.

So, all in all, there’s really nothing to get excited about here, and nothing to truly rely on as a signal for improvement. In fact, LVMH itself recently made investors aware that the worst is likely yet to come.

LVMH Is Not In A Kering-Level Crisis, But It’s Definitely Losing Its Dominant Position

For years, LVMH was considered second only to Hermes in luxury fashion. Investors grew to expect meaningful outperformance over peers like Kering (OTCPK:PPRUF) or Richemont (OTCPK:CFRHF), with consistent market share gains.

Well, this is no longer the case.

We can see LVMH losing ground to Richemont in recent quarters, something that investors couldn't have imagined just a few years ago, when the gap was so significant in the opposite direction that takeover rumors surfaced.

Typically, when businesses that are based on brand are losing their appeal, it take a very long time to climb out of that hole. LVMH is not that deep in the mud just yet, but the signs are concerning.

Valuation & Prospects

When a company like LVMH is in such a significant drawdown, the first instinct from investors is to go ahead and buy shares, blindly assuming that things will improve. One of the main themes in my articles covering such companies, the type that have a long history of being high quality long-term compounders but are now struggling, is to warn investors from blindly assuming a recovery.

Things don’t improve out of nowhere, and usually, there are signs before a turnaround starts materializing. In my mind, no signs have been shown with regards to LVMH.

In fact, we’re hearing more and more about internal issues, including CEO changes in certain units, and layoffs. Although it shows LVMH is trying to turn things around, there’s still long ways to go.

So, at EUR 483 a share, or $553 per LVMHF ADR, the stock is supposedly cheap compared to historical levels, but not so much relative to its near-term prospects.

The current valuation reflects a low-twenties multiple based on estimates that don't fully reflect the expected weakness this year. Also, when LVMH was an industry leader, there was good justification for the high-twenties multiples. That is no longer the case, as peers like Prada (OTCPK:PRDSY), Richemont, and others, are growing much faster.

So, to me, despite the drop, LVMH isn’t so obviously attractive just yet.

Conclusion

LVMH is in the midst of a borderline crisis, as it goes through the worst growth stretch in its history. This time, it's happening while some of its peers are doing far better.

The extraordinary growth in the post-pandemic boom is coming back to bite the luxury empire, which is dealing with declining desirability, weakening brand image, and a very tough macro environment.

Investors' first instinct is to go ahead and buy such significant dips in a historically-amazing company, but I'd encourage patience, as there is no sign whatsoever for a turnaround in the near term.

As such, I reiterate LVMH at a 'Hold'.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Fat Dividends Served Reality Check
data.description:
We’re posting charts for price-to-book using the Q1 2025 book values. However, we're forecasting some fairly significant changes in book value for the mortgage REITs so far during Q1 2025. Are those changes increasing or decreasing book value? Silly optimist. Of course the changes are reducing book value.

Why do you care? Because the book value represents the amount management has available to invest. When a mortgage REIT or BDC has more book value (or net asset value), it's easier to produce income. We’re constantly working on providing new updated estimates in The REIT Forum. Now you might suggest that management simply work harder to produce income. Yeah, might as well tell them to produce some unicorns. Not happening. This is simply about playing the odds.

As usual, we’ll have a bunch of charts near the end of the article.

The Big Story

Two Harbors got slapped around lately. They have notably underperformed all agency mortgage REIT peers in the last two months, last month, and last two weeks.

I addressed the weakness in our weekly update for subscribers. I’ll paraphrase it here:

“TWO’s management did a bad job during the pandemic. The board felt they could terminate the management contract for cause, removing the requirement to pay a substantial amount of money to the external manager. That resulted in a legal battle spanning several years. It appears Two Harbors will lose. In this case, a loss for Two Harbors would be a loss for shareholders as it would allow the former external manager to collect a substantial amount of money. Who doesn’t love a story where the executive who participated in overseeing the huge loss on book value in the company wins a 9 figure payday? Possibly around $140 million, maybe a bit more. Remember, we have a legal system, not a justice system.

Note: Technically, the winner would be the external manager where the executive is or was a shareholder. After all, corporations are "people" and it isn't technically the executive winning. It is the corporation he owns / owned.

This loss is not currently factored into book value for Two Harbors. It may show up as early as Q2 2025, or it may show up later. However, given the likelihood of the loss, the risk rating and price target ranges for Two Harbors have been adjusted.”

Why do we say that it appears they will lose the case? Because that’s our impression from reading the court documents. Why else would we say it?

Why is Book Value Down?

Volatility. Mortgage REITs don’t like volatility. We’ve had the whole tariff thing. Moves in bond markets. They prefer low volatility.

How hard is book value getting slammed? Well, it depends on the mortgage REIT. We have a total of one where we estimate book value is up, but the gain is under 1%. Quite a bit under 1%. OK, it’s a rounding error. The ones that are getting smacked are down hard. We can’t say when TWO would recognize the hit, so I’m going to exclude them from the following statement. The rest of the mortgage REITs have projected declines in book value per share so far during Q2 2025 of 1.4% to 14.5%. Yes, that is a big range. They have materially different portfolios. When you model out different portfolios, you get different results.

OK, I'll mention one explicitly. ARMOUR Residential REIT is among the bigger projected losers. That isn't entirely surprising. They've managed to lose book values in all kinds of situations.

What about BDCs? Book value looks very steady. By our estimates the BDCs with the biggest BV gains are up about 1% and the biggest drops are about 2%. This is based on quarter-to-date (as of last Friday) changes comparing our estimates for 5/23/2025 with the end of Q1 2025. We calculate the changes for each stock individually, but this is just the big picture.

What Do I Like?

Pizza. Dogs. Snarky articles.

I’m still more interested in the lower-risk shares. I don’t like volatility in the underlying value. I want underlying values to be relatively stable so we can capitalize on a change in valuation. Despite that, I still have some positions in higher risk shares. How has that been working out? Not as well as desired. Thanks for asking.

I also like feedback from readers. Is there a chart you would like to see added to this series? Let me know about it.

I’ll go a step further. If you have any ideas about “the best thing you could add to this series,” post it in the comments. Most of the big improvements to this series are the result of reader suggestions. It sure wasn’t my creativity. I didn’t even come up with the idea for the charts. That was a reader suggestion. It used to be a table.

All The Stocks

The charts compare the following companies and their preferred shares or baby bonds:

  • BDCs: (CSWC), (BXSL), (TSLX), (OCSL), (GAIN), (TPVG), (FSK), (MAIN), (ARCC), (GBDC), (OBDC), (SLRC)
  • Commercial mREITs: (GPMT), (FBRT), (BXMT)
  • Residential Hybrid mREITs: (MITT), (CIM), (RC), (MFA), (EFC), (NYMT)
  • Residential Agency mREITs: (NLY), (AGNC), (CHMI), (DX), (TWO), (ARR), (ORC)
  • Residential Originator and Servicer mREITs: (RITM), (PMT)

Embedded Charts

Mortgage REITs and BDCs:

Preferred shares and baby bonds:

Thanks for reading and I hope you enjoyed the charts.

Some terminology:

  • FTF = Fixed-to-floating. Share is currently fixed but will begin floating based on SOFR. We may reference LIBOR, but that's assumed to be SOFR + 0.26161%.
  • FTR = Fixed-to-reset. Share is currently fixed. It will eventually begin resetting every five years based on the five-year Treasury rate.
  • FTL = Fixed-to-lawsuit. The company decided that their FTF shares could be "fixed-to-fixed" despite clearly violating the original intent of the contract.
  • Floating = A share that was FTF, but is now floating. The dividend rate is updated every three months.

Build a foundation for steady income with REITs

As demand for key real estate sectors increases and supply fails to keep pace, 2025 presents a prime opportunity to invest in REITs, Preferreds, and BDCs.

The REIT Forum offers a proven, transparent approach with actionable insights. Led by Colorado Wealth Management Fund (aka Michael VanLoon) and an expert team, subscribers receive precise trade alerts, portfolio tracking, and exclusive articles. Strategies from The REIT Forum have consistently outperformed sector indexes for nearly a decade.

Start your risk-free two-week trial today and capitalize on the power of real estate returns.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Webtoon Entertainment: A Compelling Growth Play
data.description:
I don't know how other analysts feel, but I personally love digging into companies that operate in industries or that provide products that I am a big fan of. This intersection of interests always elates me. I remember, for instance, writing about the decision of telecommunications conglomerate AT&T (T) to sell off its Crunchyroll anime streaming service to Sony Group (SONY) a couple of years back. It was one of the favorite sagas of analysis that I have had in my career as an investor and analyst.

You can imagine my excitement in coming across another company that is tied into this industry. That is none other than Webtoon Entertainment Inc. (NASDAQ:WBTN), a firm that describes itself as a global storytelling platform dedicated to creators and audiences alike. In recent years, the company has achieved remarkable growth on the top line. Unfortunately, while some aspects of the profitability of the company have gotten better, there are still some issues from a profit standpoint. There has also been some weakness in terms of its user base.

But the good news is that the core of the company remains strong. At the end of the day, the strong cash position of the business, combined with the growth that the firm has seen recently, justifies a cautiously bullish view. Because of this, I believe that rating the company a soft Buy is appropriate.

An entertaining prospect

As I mentioned at the start of this article, the management team at Webtoon Entertainment describes the company as a global storytelling platform. However, this description is a bit nebulous. To dig in deeper, we should talk more about the platform that it offers. In essence, the platform operates by empowering and providing tools for content creators to make their own webcomics. It hosts these stories for no cost on both its website and its mobile apps. The types of content that it provides for viewers really do fall under two separate categories. The first would be Webtoon originals, which are professionally produced series curated by the company and often backed by it. Then, there is Canvas, which serves as a self-publishing space for independent creators. This gives them the opportunity to upload their work and share it with their fans.

There are different ways that the company generates revenue. For starters, it provides paid content, which involves the sale of what is on its website to its users. This includes not only the webcomics, but web novels as well. On top of this, the company generates revenue from advertising activities. This comes from displaying ads of various types. Another source of revenue for the company involves the adaptation of intellectual property into other formats such as films, streaming series, games, and merchandise. This is a category that, to me, is very interesting. Western entertainment companies such as The Walt Disney Company (DIS), Netflix (NFLX), and others have shown time and time again that taking one form of content and adapting it to other forms can be incredibly lucrative.

In recent years, the firm has achieved fantastic growth on the top line. Revenue has jumped from $1.08 billion in 2022 to $1.35 billion last year. Most of this growth has been attributable to its paid content, which saw revenue climb from $851.9 million to $1.08 billion. On a constant currency basis, the growth was even more impressive from $842.6 million to $1.15 billion. Other sources of revenue growth have been on the table as well. Over the same three-year window of time, advertising revenue expanded from $145.1 million to $166.1 million. Meanwhile, intellectual property adaptation sales increased from $82.5 million to $99.4 million.

At the core of all of this growth has been significant expansion that the company has seen in Japan. As the number of monthly active users in Japan inched up from 21.4 million to 21.9 million, and as ARPPU (average revenue per paying user) improved from $14.83 per month to $22.12 per month, total revenue coming from Japan shot up from $329 million to $594.3 million.

Even though Webtoon Entertainment focuses on animated content in general, it is important to note, when talking about Japan, that this is a massive market for animation that falls under the anime and manga category. In fact, in the firm's annual report, it mentions manga 11 times and anime twice. It does not mention, for instance, the South Korean alternative manhwa even once.

Even though Webtoon Entertainment is not restricted only to the anime and manga space, the content on them have been involved in several anime adaptations. This includes Tower of God, The God of High School, Noblesse, and the currently popular Solo Leveling, which just saw season 1 controversially win the title of Anime of the Year for 2025 that was provided by Crunchyroll. Long term, I expect that growth in Japan will continue. In the image above, you can see the total growth of the anime industry, as measured by billions of yen, between 2017 and 2023. During that time, it jumped 56.2%.

Using current foreign currency figures, the 2023 revenue generated by the industry from Japan was $23.20 billion. In 2024, the Japanese government said that it was working on creating even more growth in this industry, with the objective of growing the video games, manga, and anime industry to a $130 billion a year business by the end of this decade.

The picture has been more complicated when it comes to other markets. In South Korea, revenue has actually dropped from $445 million to $352.5 million over the last three years. Some of this was because of a reduction in monthly active users from 25.6 million to 24.4 million. However, the company also suffered a decline in ARPPU from $8.60 to $7.84. Management blamed this weakness on what it considers to be political turbulence in that country.

However, just like Japan, South Korea is facing the prospect of significant population decline that could continue this weakness moving forward. While current forecasts suggest that Japan’s population will drop from 125 million in 2024 to 63 million in the year 2100, some estimates call for South Korea experiencing a decline from 51.7 million to 21.9 million over the same window of time. Some estimates see it falling even further to 13.5 million absent changes in family formation and a willingness to become more open to immigration.

The difference between the two is that while South Korean animated and related content has become more popular in recent years, it is still a far smaller space than the Japanese anime and manga industry when talking about global reach.

The firm does have subscribers from across the world though. In all other countries combined, revenue over the last three years rose from $77.9 million to $136.2 million. The firm only saw a modest increase in the number of monthly active users during this time from $119.4 million to $120.1 million. However, ARPPU for the business ballooned from $3.19 to $6.57.

As revenue increased, the general trend on the bottom line improved. It is true that net income worsened from negative $129.9 million to negative $143.9 million. But operating cash flow improved drastically from negative $140.6 million to positive $17.9 million. If we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get an improvement from negative $70 million to positive $32.7 million. Finally, EBITDA for the company turned from negative $79.4 million to positive $68 million.

For the 2025 fiscal year, we have seen a bit of weakness, with revenue in the first quarter hitting $325.7 million compared to the $326.7 million reported one year earlier. Even though advertising revenue expanded from $37 million to $39.9 million, and even though adaptation revenue grew from $22.9 million to $25.6 million, paid content revenue dropped from $266.9 million to $260.2 million.

Some of this decline was because of a small drop in international (non-Japanese and non-South Korea) revenue from $33.2 million to $32.8 million that was caused by a substantial drop in the number of monthly active users from 123.2 million to 107.3 million that more than offset a pop in ARPPU from $6.30 to $6.50. This, management said, was because of a government ban on its Wattpad platform (which was acquired in 2021 in a deal valued at $600 million) and side effects associated with it. This appears to be because of Turkey, which banned access to it in July of 2024. There was not any official reason why Wattpad has been banned in that country. But we do know that, prior to the banning, conservative groups in the country expressed outrage over certain content on it, including content related to sexuality, violence, and the LGBTQ+ community.

The bigger problem, though, seems to have been South Korea, with revenue dropping from $90.9 million to $77 million. A drop in monthly active users from 27 million to 24.2 million, as well as a fall in ARPPU from $7.90 to $7.50 was responsible for this. The good news is that Japan continued to be a source of growth for the company, with ARPPU climbing from $22.20 to $22.30, while the number of monthly active users increased from 21.1 million to 21.9 million. That pushed overall sales for the country up from $142.2 million to $150.4 million.

The bottom line was, without exception, worse in the first quarter of 2025 than it was the same time of 2024. The firm went from generating a net profit of $6.2 million to generating a net loss of $22.4 million. Operating cash flow declined from $23.9 million to negative $18.7 million. On an adjusted basis, it dropped from $20 million to $3.9 million. Finally, EBITDA for the company plummeted from $22.2 million to $4.1 million.

As disappointing as this picture is, the company is not priced at levels that I would consider to be unreasonable. In the chart below, you can see that it is trading at a price to adjusted operating cash flow multiple of 34.6. This is truly astronomical.

However, this requires context. You see, as of this writing, Webtoon Entertainment has a market capitalization of $1.13 billion. However, the company also has $550.1 million in cash and cash equivalents on its books. That is a tremendous amount of capital considering that it has no debt at this time. When you look at the picture through the lens of the EV to EBITDA multiple, as shown above, you can see that it is trading at a multiple of just 9.1. For a company that is growing nicely, and that has attractive potential when it comes to both Japan and markets outside of South Korea, I would say that this makes it an appealing prospect.

Takeaway

Based on all the data currently available, it seems to me as though the market is misjudging Webtoon Entertainment. Yes, the firm has certain weak spots to it. South Korea is particularly problematic right now. But when you consider its strength in Japan and the opportunity for management to further grow in international markets, including through ARPPU expansion, I believe that it makes for an attractive, though admittedly speculative, Buy candidate.

data.title: Meta: Seamless Integration Of AI Services Can Change The Game
data.description:
Meta’s (NASDAQ:META) recent earnings improved the sentiment towards the stock as the company was able to beat both the revenue and earnings estimate by a good margin. The EPS came in at $6.43 and it was $1.22 higher than the estimates. One of the key metrics to look for in the recent earnings was the price per ad. This metric grew by 10% YoY as advertisers were ready to pay higher price for more targeted ads. Meta is using new ad tools to increase the performance of ads. This trend could continue for the next few quarters as better AI tools are built for advertisers. Meta has also recently launched its standalone AI app as it competes with ChatGPT and other AI platforms. The high customer engagement and good monetization record gives Meta a big edge over other competitors as the race for getting more customers on AI platforms increases. In the previous article, it was mentioned that capex worries might be reduced as we focus on key growth drivers.

Meta also announced an increase in its capex spending to a range of $64 billion to $72 billion for 2025. Any slowdown in EPS growth could lead to more focus on capex and build a negative sentiment towards the stock. Meta continues to show massive losses for Reality Labs. Both higher capex and Reality Labs losses could be a headwind for the stock in the next few quarters. However, Meta’s strong monetization ability and modest multiple should help deliver further upside to the stock. Meta is trading at only 23 times the EPS estimate for fiscal year ending 2025 which is quite reasonable when we look at forward EPS growth projections and the potential of monetization of new AI tools and apps.

King of monetization in the AI era

Meta has shown that it can deliver rapid monetization of its AI investments by building attractive AI tools which help advertisers gain better returns for their spending. This has helped the company deliver a good trajectory for its price per ad metric. In the recent quarter, the price per ad metric grew by 10% YoY compared to 5% growth in ad impressions. There is an upper limit on how many ads can be delivered on the platform before users start to show lower engagement. However, there is almost no upper limit for the price per ad metric. If Meta continues to deliver better targeted ads for advertisers, the price per ad metric could show a strong upward trend for the next few quarters.

Figure: Key metrics of Meta in the recent quarter. Source: Meta Filings

Most of the big tech companies have made massive investments for their AI business. However, Meta has taken a lead in showing a rapid monetization of new AI tools. It is likely that the future market share of AI platforms will depend more on the monetization ability of the players instead of how good the AI model is. Over the last few weeks and months, we have continued to see new AI models being launched regularly.

Figure: Recent AI model ranking. Source: Artificial Analysis

It should be noted that Meta’s Llama does not have a good ranking in the AI leaderboard of new AI models. Even Alibaba (BABA) has gained a higher ranking than Meta. However, Meta has the resources to improve its ranking over the next few quarters. A more important aspect is that Meta has the customer engagement on its platform, which could be a massive moat for its AI tools.

Few AI users could tell the difference between an AI model that has the top rank and another that has the 20th rank. Most users are looking for seamless usage of AI tools. Meta can provide this from its social networking platforms.

Tailwind due to network effect

Meta’s Daily Active People metric also showed a 6% YoY growth to 3.43 billion in the recent quarter. In January 2025, Meta mentioned that 700 million were now using Meta AI. It is highly likely that Meta could reach over a 1 billion users for its Meta AI platform by the end of this year. The company has also launched a standalone AI app to battle other AI platforms. The winner in this AI race is yet to be decided but Meta has a very good potential of attracting a significant customer base and advertising revenue due to the network effect of its massive social media ecosystem.

In August 2024, Reuters reported that ChatGPT user base has increased to 200 million. Recent reports mention that ChatGPT’s user base has increased to 400 million or doubling in 6 months. However, ChatGPT still faces a key issue. Despite massive fundraising, it has to compete with big tech companies that have much higher cash flows. ChatGPT also does not have the network effect of Meta. It is highly likely that new AI models will be launched in the next few months which will change the ranking. ChatGPT, DeepSeek, Perplexity and other AI platforms have created a good brand recall in the last few quarters but Meta could still beat them in terms of customer engagement by providing more seamless integration of AI tools within its social media ecosystem.

Figure: Growth of ChatGPT in last few quarters. Source: Techcrunch

Headwinds due to higher capex and Reality Labs

Despite the good EPS growth and positive trends in the core social media platform, Meta still faces headwinds due to high capex and losses in Reality Labs. Meta recently announced another hike in capex to as much as $72 billion. All the recent focus of Wall Street on good quarterly numbers and improvement in macro conditions. However, Meta and other Big Tech players will eventually face questions about their massive capex spend. Even Buffett recently mentioned that a great business is one that requires less capex while talking about massive investment in AI infrastructure. It is an open question if the capex on AI can deliver the returns.

Figure: Recent impact on free cash flow. Source: Meta Filings

Meta’s FCF declined from $12.5 billion in the year-ago quarter to $10.3 billion in the recent quarter. This is despite a good increase in profitability. The main reason is the over 100% increase in purchase of property and equipment to $12.9 billion from $6.4 billion in the year-ago quarter. Wall Street can get more cautious over these massive spend over the next few quarters which can have a negative impact on the valuation multiple.

Another negative headwind for Meta is the higher losses in Reality Labs. Meta has been reporting annualized losses of over $15 billion in Reality Labs for the past few years. There have been successful products like the smart glasses and Meta is still ahead in this segment despite the entry of Apple (AAPL). However, the monetization roadmap for Reality Labs is a lot less clearer compared to AI investments.

Figure: Reality Labs losses continue to be high. Source: Meta Filings

Still a good buy after the recent bullish run

In the last few weeks, Meta stock has climbed by over a third. This has helped the stock finally move to positive territory in YTD. In the recent earnings, Meta mentioned tariffs 0 times compared to 29 times by Apple. This shows that the impact of macro headwinds could be lower on the company compared to some other big tech players with more integrated supply chain.

The stock is trading at 23 times the EPS estimate for fiscal year ending Dec 2026 and 20 times the EPS estimate for fiscal year ending Dec 2027. This is still a modest number when we look at the historical average multiple for Meta. As mentioned above, the company also has a good potential to deliver better AI monetization compared to other platforms. This should be an additional revenue stream and improve the growth runway for the company.

Figure: Forward EPS estimates of Meta in the next few years. Source: Seeking Alpha

Meta has made share repurchase of $13 billion in the recent quarter.

The buyback pace of Meta fluctuates compared to a more steady pace of Apple and Alphabet (GOOG). However, the company has enough resources to ramp up buybacks which can improve EPS growth and provide a better floor to the stock price.

A lot depends on how Meta's AI app and the engagement with its AI tool play out in the next few quarters. As mentioned above, despite the low AI ranking, Meta has a massive network effect and can provide customers with seamless integration of AI services which competitors like ChatGPT or Perplexity cannot offer.

Investor Takeaway

Meta’s latest earnings have improved sentiment towards the stock. The AI tools have helped increase price per ad metric. Meta has a big edge in the AI race despite its Llama model having a lower ranking than ChatGPT, Gemini and other players. The massive network effect of the social media platforms increases the usage of Meta’s AI tools. The management has already announced that they intend to be the first AI platform to reach a billion years in 2025.

Meta’s high capex and massive losses in Reality Labs could become a headwind for the stock sentiment in the next few quarters. The FCF has already seen a dip due to capex increase. Despite these issues, the stock has a good upside due to modest valuation and higher monetization potential in the near term making it a Buy.

data.title: Arrowhead Pharmaceuticals: A Reluctant Sell Rating Explained (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Investment Overview - Shares Down Despite Lucrative Sarepta Deal

Since I gave Arrowhead Pharmaceuticals, Inc.'s (NASDAQ:ARWR) shares a "Hold" rating in October 2024, they have fallen in value by >15%, and since my March 2024 "Sell rating", down >40%.

In my last note, I focused on what I suggested were Arrowhead's three most important assets:

a single wholly-owned asset, plozasiran, Pharma giant Amgen Inc. (AMGN) partnered olpasiran, and fazirsiran, partnered with Japanese Pharma giant Takeda.

As a reminder, Arrowhead's drug development efforts center around RNA-interference, designed to "induce rapid, deep and durable knockdown of target genes" (source: fiscal Q2 2025 quarterly report / 10Q submission). The company lists the advantages of its "TRiM" ("targeted RNAi molecule") platform as follows:

simplified manufacturing and reduced costs; multiple routes of administration including subcutaneous injection and inhaled administration; the ability to target multiple tissue types including liver, lung, central nervous system ("CNS"), muscle, and adipose tissue; and the potential for improved safety and reduced risk of intracellular buildup, because there are fewer metabolites from smaller, simpler molecules.

Arrowhead stock hit a five-year low price of >$10 at the end of March this year, although it has recovered slightly to trade at $16 at the time of writing, giving it a market cap valuation of $2.2bn. Cash and investments totaling $1.1bn were reported when the company announced its fiscal Q2 2025 earnings earlier this month.

The biggest news announced since my last note is a licensing and collaboration agreement struck with Sarepta Therapeutics (SRPT). Arrowhead noted in a press release:

Upon closing, Arrowhead receives a $500 million upfront payment and $325 million through the purchase by Sarepta of Arrowhead common stock priced at $27.25 per share. Arrowhead will also receive $250 million to be paid in annual installments of $50 million over 5 years. Arrowhead also has the potential to receive $300 million in near-term payments associated with the continued enrollment of certain cohorts of a Phase 1/2 study of ARO-DM1, which Arrowhead is on track to achieve during the next 12 months.

Arrowhead is eligible to receive development milestone payments of between $110 million and $410 million per program and sales milestone payments of between $500 million and $700 million per program. Arrowhead is also eligible to receive tiered royalties on commercial sales up to the low double digits.

This deal has arguably taken on even greater significance since Sarepta has reported a patient death related to its gene therapy drug Elevidys, approved to treat Duchenne Muscular Dystrophy, leading to a downgrade in full-year 2025 guidance - Sarepta shares are down nearly 70% year-to-date.

One of the four clinical stage candidates Arrowhead expects to develop in partnership with Sarepta targets type 1 myotonic dystrophy ("DM1"), while a second, facioscapulohumeral muscular dystrophy type 1. The third and fourth target idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis, and spinocerebellar ataxia 2 ("SCA2"). All four drugs are at the Phase 1/2 clinical development stage. The deal also includes development of four preclinical candidates.

Arrowhead - Progress Since My Last Note - Plozasiran NDA, Takeda Partnered Fazirsiran Progress Encouraging

Arrowhead shares an overview of its current drug development pipeline in an April corporate presentation, as follows:

Arrowhead's New Drug Application ("NDA") requesting approval for plozasiran has been accepted by the Food and Drug Agency ("FDA"), who have set a decision date of November 18th, 2025 (decisions occasionally arrive before that date). The company has also completed its marketing application for approval in Europe. As I wrote in my last note:

Plozasiran uses RNAi to reduce the production of the protein Apolipoprotein-CIII (ApoC3), ApoC3 being a protein produced by liver cells that inhibits the formation and clearance of lipids and lipoproteins, including triglycerides.

Unlike statins, which target LDL cholesterol, plozasiran targets triglycerides, another form of "bad" cholesterol. The drug aced its Phase 3 study, achieving median triglyceride reductions of -78% and -73% at 12 months, against a median of 7% for patients using placebo.

FCS represents a very small patient population, however, so while an approval would be a significant achievement for Arrowhead, it would not be a lucrative one. As we can see below however, the company has expansive plans for its most advanced asset.

As Arrowhead's CEO Christopher Anzalone commented on its recent Q2 2025 earnings call, in relation to migrating from FCS to severe hypertriglyceridemia ("SHTG"):

we view this as just the beginning. SHASTA-3, SHASTA-4 and MUIR 3 are Phase III studies designed to support a supplemental NDA and other applications on a global basis to enable us to treat the broader SHTG population.

These studies are moving rapidly and we believe they could be fully enrolled this summer. We are also in the process of initiating SHASTA-5, which is an outcome study to specifically evaluate the risk reduction of acute pancreatitis in high-risk patients with SHTG. We think this is an innovative strategy to potentially demonstrate meaningful value for patients, physicians and payers.

There are presently no drugs approved to treat SHTG. However, Ionis Pharmaceuticals (IONS), whose drug candidate olezarsen is now approved to treat FCS as Tryngolza, has achieved statistically significant results in a Phase 3 study of the same drug in SHTG - a placebo-adjusted 61% and 58% reduction in triglyceride ("TG") levels at 6 months with the 80 mg and 50 mg monthly doses, also meeting all key secondary endpoints.

Ionis has promised more Phase 3 data, and potentially, a regulatory filing for approval in SHTG this year, which puts Arrowhead a long way behind - 12-18 months, I'd estimate, and needing to generate some very strong data to compete successfully against its more established rival. Ionis has commercialized several drugs, while Arrowhead has yet to commercialize any.

Turning to Olpasiran, I noted in my last post that Arrowhead has sold its rights to royalties on sales of that drug, to Royalty Pharma (RPRX), and while there are still ~$535m of milestone payments on the table, these may be based on hard to achieve sales milestones and therefore never be collected. Olpasiran has progressed into a Phase 3 study in Atherosclerotic Cardiovascular Disease ("ASCVD"), but barely warrants a mention in Arrowhead's latest earnings call, or in its corporate presentation.

One asset I neglected to cover in much detail in my last note was Pharma giant GSK (GSK) partnered metabolic dysfunction-associated steatotic liver disease ("MASH") candidate GSK4532990. According to Arrowhead's latest 10Q:

The Company is eligible for an additional payment of $100.0 million upon achieving the first patient dosed in a Phase 3 trial. Furthermore, should the Phase 3 trial read out positively, and the potential new medicine receives regulatory approval in major markets, the deal provides for commercial milestone payments to the Company of up to $190.0 million at first commercial sale, and up to $590.0 million in sales-related milestone payments. The Company is further eligible to receive tiered royalties on net product sales in a range of mid-teens to twenty percent.

Once again, however, the prospects for this drug appear to be unclear. GSK lists it as being in a Phase 2b study in its 2024 annual report, and in 2023, Arrowhead received a $30m payment after the Phase 2b study initiation. Whether GSK will opt to continue development - according to clinicaltrials.gov, the study's estimated primary completion date is January 2026, and estimated completion data April 2027.

There is more clarity on the Takeda partnered liver disease candidate Fazirsiran, however. According to CEO Christopher Anzalone:

Fazirsiran is partnered with Takeda and they have publicly guided the Phase III studies could complete enrollment this year. They are two-year studies to primary endpoint.

While this is partnered, our economics are substantial with 50/50 profit share in the U.S., 20% to 25% royalties ex-US and up to $527 million of remaining milestones. (source: earnings call).

In December last year, Takeda cited Fazirsiran as one of four products it hoped would achieve $10bn - $20bn in peak revenues combined, referring to the drug as an:

investigational RNA interference ("RNAI") therapy that stops the production of misfolded abnormal protein Z-AAT directly addressing the pathology of alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency liver disease (AATD-LD).

It's unclear what share of that $10bn - $20bn figure fazirsiran would claim, but Takeda would be unlikely to include the drug unless it had, at the very least, "blockbuster" (>$1bn revenues per annum) potential.

Pivot To Obesity? CKD Program Intrigues

Obesity drugs are all the rage in pharmaceutical circles thanks to the incredible success of Novo Nordisk's (NVO) Wegovy and Eli Lilly's (LLY) Zepbound, two drugs expected to form the cornerstone of a >$100bn market by 2030, even if pressure on pricing of these drugs has intensified recently.

Could Arrowhead be part of the obesity treatment landscape by 2030? According to Arrowhead's Chief of Discovery & Translational Medicine James Hamilton:

ARO-INHBE and ARO-ALK7. ARO-INHBE is designed to reduce expression of Activin E, which is a ligand for adipose ALK7, while ARO-ALK7 is designed to reduce expression of the ALK7 receptor itself, both of which are involved in regulating adipose storage of fats. These programs both have the potential to reduce visceral fat mass while simultaneously preserving lean mass, which we demonstrated in preclinical models and are now evaluating in clinical studies.

Frankly, it's generally unwise to attach much value to drug candidates that have shared only preclinical data, as there is (currently) no substitute for in-human studies. Regarding that, management says that:

We anticipate that some initial data may be available for ARO-INHBE around the end of 2025 and potentially for ARO-ALK7 shortly thereafter.

Finally, management also provided some promising updates in relation to ARO-C3, targeting complement mediated diseases. A large number of complement system targeting drugs have been approved, including, e.g., soliris - indicated for paroxysmal nocturnal hemoglobinuria / myasthenia gravis and generating >$2.5bn of revenues last year, and Izervay and Syfovre, the eye disease drugs that earned combined revenues of >$750m last year, and Fabhalta, a drug developed by Novartis (NVS) approved to treat PNH and IgAN, the liver diseases.

Arrowhead revealed (on its Q2 earnings call) that:

ARO-C3 also led to an important improvement in proteinuria, with a mean reduction in spot UPCR of 41% and maximum individual reduction of 89% from baseline by week 24.

Based on my recent analysis of Vera Therapeutics (VERA), such a figure would potentially secure a commercial approval for Arrowhead, provided the safety profile was robust, although target markets (liver and kidney) are becoming crowded.

Final Thoughts - Still A Tricky Value Proposition For Me

Arrowhead stock made a meaningful jump after Q2 earnings were announced. The company has a substantial amount of cash - >$1bn, and I own a (very) small holding of shares, purchased many years ago, however my optimism back then has not been justified.

My fear with Arrownhead is that in every target market, the company is a little behind a competitor of real significance - Ionis in SHTG, Novartis in kidney disease, numerous companies in neuromuscular conditions - while its partnerships don't inspire too much confidence.

The Sarepta deal could be worth $11bn, the company has suggested, but with Sarepta now struggling in the wake of its Elevidys setbacks, and studies at the earliest stage, progress could be painfully slow. GSK and Amgen partnered assets either don't pay out much if successful, or appear to be drifting, although Takeda offers a glimmer of hope.

Obesity is becoming a crowded market for development, also - I count >30 GLP-1 agonist drugs in development, and the bar set by Wegovy / Zepbound is exceptionally high.

Arrowhead stock has halved in value over a five-year period, and I would not be too surprised if it halved again over the next five years. Accumulated deficit stands at $1.43bn, and the company reported operating income of $(601m) last year.

As such, while I don't think Arrowhead is necessarily a bad company, and it technology could still deliver an outstanding drug candidates, I don't it has done so to date, and therefore I reach the conclusion that shares do look overvalued at current price of $17, despite the price paid by Sarepta only a few months ago. With their superior experience and similar approach to Arrowhead, I'd rate Ionis and Alnylam (ALNY) as the horses to back in the RNAi field.
